abstract: The static ``train then deploy" paradigm fundamentally limits Large Language Models (LLMs) from dynamically adapting their weights in response to continuous streams of new information inherent in real-world tasks. Test-Time Training (TTT) offers a compelling alternative by updating a subset of model parameters (fast weights) at inference time, yet its potential in the current LLM ecosystem is hindered by critical barriers including architectural incompatibility, computational inefficiency and misaligned fast weight objectives for language modeling. In this work, we introduce In-Place Test-Time Training (In-Place TTT), a framework that seamlessly endows LLMs with Test-Time Training ability. In-Place TTT treats the final projection matrix of the ubiquitous MLP blocks as its adaptable fast weights, enabling a ``drop-in" enhancement for LLMs without costly retraining from scratch. Furthermore, we replace TTT's generic reconstruction objective with a tailored, theoretically-grounded objective explicitly aligned with the Next-Token-Prediction task governing autoregressive language modeling. This principled objective, combined with an efficient chunk-wise update mechanism, results in a highly scalable algorithm compatible with context parallelism. Extensive experiments validate our framework's effectiveness: as an in-place enhancement, it enables a 4B-parameter model to achieve superior performance on tasks with contexts up to 128k, and when pretrained from scratch, it consistently outperforms competitive TTT-related approaches. Ablation study results further provide deeper insights on our design choices. Collectively, our results establish In-Place TTT as a promising step towards a paradigm of continual learning in LLMs.

citations.fullCitation: [47] Yu Sun, Xiaolong Wang, Zhuang Liu, John Miller, Alexei A. Efros, and Moritz Hardt. Test-time training with self-supervision for generalization under distribution shifts. In Proceedings of the 37th International Conference on Machine Learning , volume 119 of Proceedings of Machine Learning Research, pages 9229–9248. PMLR, 2020. URL https://proceedings.mlr.press/v119/sun20b.html.
citations.justification: This is a foundational paper that introduced the Test-Time Training (TTT) paradigm, which is the core concept that the main paper builds upon. The authors of 'In-Place TTT' aim to adapt and resolve the critical barriers of this original TTT mechanism for modern Large Language Models.
citations.title: Test-time training with self-supervision for generalization under distribution shifts

citations.fullCitation: [43] Imanol Schlag, Kazuki Irie, and Jürgen Schmidhuber. Linear transformers are secretly fast weight programmers. In International Conference on Machine Learning, pages 9355–9366. PMLR, 2021.
citations.justification: This paper provides the theoretical underpinning for the 'fast weights' mechanism, a central component of Test-Time Training. It is also directly related to the DeltaNet baseline, against which 'In-Place TTT' is experimentally compared, making it crucial for understanding the architectural context.
citations.title: Linear transformers are secretly fast weight programmers

citations.fullCitation: [67] Tianyuan Zhang, Sai Bi, Yicong Hong, Kai Zhang, Fujun Luan, Songlin Yang, Kalyan Sunkavalli, William T. Freeman, and Hao Tan. Test-time training done right. arXiv preprint arXiv:2505.23884, 2025. URL https://arxiv.org/abs/2505.23884.
citations.justification: This paper introduces Large Chunk Test-Time Training (LaCT), which serves as a key competitive baseline in the pre-training experiments. The main paper positions its contributions, such as the LM-aligned objective, as an improvement over the generic reconstruction targets used in this and other prior TTT works.
citations.title: Test-time training done right

citations.fullCitation: [48] Yu Sun, Xinhao Li, Karan Dalal, Jiarui Xu, Arjun Vikram, Genghan Zhang, Yann Dubois, Xinlei Chen, Xiaolong Wang, Sanmi Koyejo, Tatsunori Hashimoto, and Carlos Guestrin. Learning to (learn at test time): Rnns with expressive hidden states. arXiv preprint arXiv:2407.04620, 2024. URL https://arxiv.org/abs/2407.04620.
citations.justification: This work is frequently cited alongside the original TTT paper to define the canonical TTT mechanism and its extension to language tasks. The main paper explicitly addresses the limitations of this approach, such as its inherent sequential nature and architectural incompatibility, which 'In-Place TTT' is designed to overcome.
citations.title: Learning to (learn at test time): Rnns with expressive hidden states

intermediateReport:
**Research Report: In-Place Test-Time Training**

This report provides a detailed analysis of the paper "In-Place Test-Time Training" by Feng et al., focusing on its contributions to dynamic adaptation in Large Language Models (LLMs).

---

### 1. Authors and Institution(s)

The authors of this research are Guhao Feng, Shengjie Luo, Kai Hua, Ge Zhang, Di He, Wenhao Huang, and Tianle Cai.
Their affiliations include:
*   ByteDance Seed (Guhao Feng, Shengjie Luo, Kai Hua, Ge Zhang, Wenhao Huang, Tianle Cai)
*   Peking University (Guhao Feng, Di He)

Guhao Feng and Shengjie Luo contributed equally to this work. Di He and Wenhao Huang are the corresponding authors.

---

### 2. How This Work Fits into the Broader Research Landscape

Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated advanced capabilities across various complex tasks, primarily operating under a static "train then deploy" paradigm. This paradigm, however, inherently limits models from dynamically adapting their learned weights in response to continuous streams of new information or evolving real-world contexts. Once deployed, the model's knowledge remains fixed, hindering its ability to process long-horizon, dynamic tasks and learn continuously.

Current approaches to mitigate these limitations include in-context learning, which maintains past input tokens within the context window. However, this method is constrained by the quadratic complexity of the attention mechanism, limiting the maximum context length. This bottleneck has spurred architectural research focused on efficiently extending context windows, such as sparse attention mechanisms (e.g., Longformer, Sparse Transformers), linear-time attention variants (e.g., linear attention, Gated Linear Attention), and State-Space Models (SSMs). These efforts primarily focus on making static models more efficient over longer sequences rather than enabling dynamic adaptation of model parameters.

Test-Time Training (TTT) represents a distinct paradigm that directly addresses the static nature of deployed models. TTT involves updating a small subset of model parameters, known as fast weights, on the fly during inference for each new input. These fast weights are intended to compress and internalize contextual information, serving as an expressive, online evolving state. While TTT has shown promise across modalities, its application to the LLM ecosystem faces specific challenges:
1.  **Architectural Incompatibility**: Many existing TTT methods introduce specialized layers that necessitate expensive pretraining from scratch, which is impractical for large, billion-parameter LLMs built on standard Transformer blocks.
2.  **Computational Inefficiency**: The canonical per-token update rule of TTT is sequential, limiting parallelism and creating a bottleneck for high-throughput language modeling. Although chunk-wise acceleration has been explored, TTT's role as a primary token mixer often requires small chunk sizes to maintain performance.
3.  **Misaligned Learning Objectives**: The prevalent use of generic self-supervised reconstruction objectives for fast weight updates in TTT is not explicitly tailored for the causal, Next-Token Prediction (NTP) task fundamental to autoregressive language models.

This work, In-Place Test-Time Training (In-Place TTT), aims to bridge these gaps by providing a framework that seamlessly integrates TTT capabilities into existing LLMs, addressing the aforementioned barriers through architectural repurposing, efficient update mechanisms, and an objective specifically aligned with language modeling. It seeks to transition LLMs towards a paradigm of continual learning and dynamic adaptation.

---

### 3. Key Objectives and Motivation

The primary objective of this research is to introduce a framework, In-Place Test-Time Training (In-Place TTT), that seamlessly endows Large Language Models (LLMs) with Test-Time Training (TTT) capabilities. The motivation stems from the inherent limitations of the static "train then deploy" paradigm for LLMs, which prevents them from dynamically adapting to continuous streams of new information and evolving contexts during real-world inference.

The specific key objectives and motivations are detailed as follows:

*   **Addressing Architectural Incompatibility for LLMs**:
    *   **Motivation**: Existing TTT methods often introduce specialized layers that are not part of standard Transformer architectures. Integrating these typically requires costly pretraining of the entire model from scratch, which is prohibitive for billion-parameter LLMs. Such modifications also risk disrupting the carefully learned properties of pre-trained attention mechanisms.
    *   **Objective**: To enable a "drop-in" enhancement for LLMs without requiring fundamental architectural modifications or costly retraining from scratch. The aim is to leverage existing components within the Transformer architecture as fast weights, preserving the integrity and utility of pre-trained weights.

*   **Overcoming Computational Inefficiency**:
    *   **Motivation**: The canonical TTT mechanism relies on per-token updates, which are inherently sequential and bottleneck the massive parallelism capabilities of modern accelerators (GPUs/TPUs). While chunk-wise updates have been proposed, existing TTT layers, when acting as primary token mixers, often require small chunk sizes, limiting throughput.
    *   **Objective**: To design an adaptation mechanism that is computationally efficient and scalable on modern parallel hardware. This involves moving beyond fine-grained per-token updates to a more efficient chunk-wise update strategy that can effectively leverage large chunk sizes without compromising performance.

*   **Aligning Fast Weight Objectives with Language Modeling**:
    *   **Motivation**: Prior TTT work often employs generic self-supervised reconstruction objectives, where fast weights learn to associate input token representations with themselves. While this allows for information storage, its direct relevance and optimality for the causal Next-Token Prediction (NTP) task central to autoregressive LLMs are not guaranteed. A generic objective may not effectively capture the predictive dependencies crucial for language understanding.
    *   **Objective**: To replace generic reconstruction with a tailored, theoretically-grounded objective explicitly aligned with the Next-Token Prediction task. This objective aims to encourage fast weights to store predictively useful information for future tokens, thereby enhancing the LLM's dynamic adaptation for language generation.

By addressing these barriers, In-Place TTT seeks to unlock the full potential of Test-Time Training within the current LLM ecosystem, making dynamic adaptation a practical and effective capability for improving LLM performance on long-horizon, evolving tasks.

---

### 4. Methodology and Approach

The In-Place Test-Time Training (In-Place TTT) framework is designed to integrate Test-Time Training (TTT) capabilities into LLMs by addressing architectural incompatibility, computational inefficiency, and objective misalignment. The methodology comprises repurposing existing architectural components, implementing an efficient chunk-wise update mechanism, and developing a language modeling-aligned learning objective.

**4.1. Overall Framework: Repurposing MLP Blocks for In-Place Adaptation**
Instead of introducing new, specialized TTT layers that necessitate pretraining from scratch, In-Place TTT repurposes existing Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) blocks within the Transformer architecture.
*   **Architectural Integration**: The standard Transformer's MLP block, often a gated MLP `O = ϕ(HWᵀ_gate) ⊙ (HWᵀ_up) Wᵀ_down`, is utilized.
*   **Fast Weights Designation**: The final projection matrix, `W_down`, of the MLP block is designated as the adaptable "fast weights." The input projections `W_up` and `W_gate` are kept frozen as "slow weights."
*   **Mechanism**: By exclusively updating `W_down` in-place during inference, the framework preserves the model's original architecture. This design enables a "drop-in" enhancement without requiring modifications to the pre-trained model's core structure or expensive retraining from scratch. This approach is motivated by the view of MLP blocks as a form of key-value memory for general knowledge.

**4.2. Efficient Adaptation with Chunk-Wise Updates**
To address the computational inefficiency of sequential per-token updates, In-Place TTT employs a scalable chunk-wise update strategy.
*   **Decoupling from Attention**: Unlike prior TTT methods that often aimed to replace attention, In-Place TTT complements the existing attention mechanism. This allows for larger chunk sizes without the performance degradation typically associated with small chunks when TTT acts as the sole token mixer.
*   **Process**:
    1.  Given intermediate activations `Z` (derived from `ϕ(HWᵀ_gate) ⊙ (HWᵀ_up)`) and value targets `V`, both are partitioned into `k` non-overlapping chunks of size `C`.
    2.  For each chunk `i`, an **Apply Operation** uses the current fast weights `W_down(i)` to compute the output `O[i] = Z[i](W_down(i))ᵀ`.
    3.  Subsequently, an **Update Operation** adjusts the fast weights `W_down(i)` using `Z[i]` as keys and `V[i]` as values. This is performed via a single gradient descent step with a loss function `L` and learning rate `η`: `W_down(i+1) = W_down(i) - η∇_W L(Z[i](W_down(i))ᵀ, V[i])`.
*   **Scalability**: This chunk-wise strategy is designed to leverage modern parallel hardware, allowing the processing of large blocks of tokens concurrently and improving throughput.

**4.3. LM-Aligned Objective**
The performance of In-Place TTT is further enhanced by a novel learning objective explicitly tailored for language modeling's Next-Token Prediction (NTP) task.
*   **Motivation**: Traditional TTT reconstruction objectives, where `v` is derived from the current input token `x`, primarily encourage memorization of the current token's representation. This is considered suboptimal for autoregressive language models, which require predicting future tokens.
*   **Objective Definition**: The target `v` is specified to include future token information. Formally, the target `V̂ = Conv1D(X₀)W_target`, where `X₀` is the token embedding, `Conv1D(·)` is a 1D Convolution operator, and `W_target` is a trainable projection matrix.
    *   `Conv1D(·)` controls the extent of future token information included. For instance, a next-token target can be achieved by setting `W_target` as identity and the `Conv1D` kernel to extract only the next token.
*   **Loss Function**: The chosen loss function is the negative Frobenius inner product, `L(·, ·) = -⟨·, ·⟩_F`.
*   **Gradient Update**: Under this loss, the gradient update rule for the fast weights in the chunk-wise mechanism is `W_down(i) = W_down(i-1) + η V̂[i]ᵀ Z[i]`.

**4.4. Theoretical Analysis**
The paper provides a theoretical analysis grounding the benefits of the LM-Aligned objective within the canonical induction head setting, critical for in-context learning.
*   **Setup**: An input sequence contains a key-value pair `(k*, v*)` at `t*`. At a later query position `n`, `k*` reappears. The model must predict `v*` as `x_n+1`.
*   **Comparison**: The analysis compares the logit-wise effect of the LM-Aligned target (`V_LM_t = E_x_t+1`) against a reconstruction target (`V_rec_t = E_x_t`).
*   **Assumptions**:
    1.  Approximate orthogonality of embeddings: Distinct token embeddings are nearly orthogonal.
    2.  Key-query alignment: Intermediate activations for an aligned key-query pair exhibit positive correlation.
*   **Theorem 1**: With the LM-Aligned target, the expected change in logits `E[Δℓ_n[v*]]` for the correct next token `v*` is guaranteed to increase (lower bound `≥ λ_lr ⋅ c_norm^2 ⋅ c_align`), while logits for other tokens remain approximately unchanged. In contrast, with the reconstruction target, the expected change in `E[Δℓ_n[v*]]` for the correct token is negligible. This theoretically supports the LM-Aligned objective's effectiveness for predictive tasks.

**4.5. Implementation Details**
*   **Context Parallelism (CP)**: The associative nature of the update rule `W_down(i) = W_down(i-1) + η V̂[i]ᵀ Z[i]` enables efficient implementation with CP.
    1.  Parallel computation of intermediate activations `Z[i]` and individual chunk updates `ΔW_i = V̂[i]ᵀ Z[i]`.
    2.  A parallel prefix sum operation `S_i = Σ_{j<i} ΔW_j` aggregates updates for each chunk.
    3.  Parallel computation of effective fast weights `W_down(i-1) = W_down(0) + ηS_i` and corresponding outputs `O_i`.
*   **Causality and Boundary Handling**: Causal padding in the `Conv1D` operator ensures that each chunk's update delta does not contain future information. Fast weights are reset to their pre-trained state at document boundaries to prevent context leakage across independent sequences.
*   **Initialization**: When integrated into a pre-trained model, the newly introduced `Conv1D` kernel and `W_target` projection matrix are initialized such that the initial TTT update `ΔW_down` is negligible. This ensures the model starts with its original pre-trained behavior, with the TTT mechanism gradually learning to produce meaningful updates during continual training.

---

### 5. Main Findings and Results

The experiments conducted aimed to validate the effectiveness of In-Place TTT as a drop-in enhancement for pre-trained LLMs, its performance when pre-trained from scratch compared to baselines, and the impact of its key design choices.

**5.1. In-Place TTT as a Drop-in Enhancement for Pre-trained LLMs**
*   **Setup**: The Qwen3-4B-Base model (original context window 32k) was continually trained with In-Place TTT (or as a baseline) on a curriculum including 128k context lengths. Evaluation was performed on the RULER benchmark with context lengths up to 256k. RoPE adaptation using YaRN was applied for long contexts.
*   **Results (Qwen3-4B-Base)**: In-Place TTT demonstrated a consistent and widening performance advantage over the baseline Qwen3-4B-Base as sequence length increased. Substantial gains were observed at 64k and 128k context lengths. This advantage was maintained during extrapolation to 256k context, indicating improved generalization. For instance, at 128k context, In-Place TTT achieved 77.0% accuracy compared to the baseline's 74.8%, and at 256k, it reached 43.9% versus 41.7%.
*   **Results (Extension to Other Models)**: In-Place TTT was also applied to LLaMA-3.1-8B and Qwen3-14B-Base. It consistently improved RULER scores across all context lengths for both models, with gains being more pronounced at longer contexts (e.g., +2.1% at 64k for LLaMA-3.1-8B and +2.7% at 64k for Qwen3-14B-Base). The improvements were orthogonal to RoPE extension techniques like YaRN.

**5.2. Pre-training from Scratch: A Comparative Analysis**
*   **Setup (500M and 1.5B scales)**: In-Place TTT was benchmarked against existing TTT-related approaches and efficient attention methods (standard Transformer with Sliding Window Attention (SWA), Gated Linear Attention (GLA), DeltaNet, and Large Chunk Test-Time Training (LaCT)) on models pretrained from scratch with 32k context lengths. All models were trained for 20B (500M) or 60B (1.5B) tokens. Evaluation used Sliding Window Perplexity on Pile and Proof-Pile-2 datasets.
*   **Results (500M and 1.5B models)**: In-Place TTT consistently achieved lower validation perplexity than all competitive baselines across varying context lengths (2k to 32k). Its performance steadily improved with increasing context, suggesting effective compression and utilization of contextual information.
*   **Setup (4B scale)**: 4B-parameter models with Full Attention and SWA were compared against their In-Place TTT-enhanced counterparts. These models were trained for 120B tokens with an 8k context length. Evaluation included common sense reasoning benchmarks (HellaSwag, ARC, MMLU, PIQA) and the long-context RULER benchmark.
*   **Results (4B models)**: In-Place TTT consistently improved performance across most common sense reasoning tasks for both Full Attention and SWA Transformers. Significant gains were observed on the long-context RULER benchmark, with the RULER-16k score improving from 6.58 to 19.99 for the Full Attention Transformer and the RULER-8k score increasing from 9.91 to 26.80 for the SWA model.

**5.3. Ablation Studies: On the Impact of Key Design Choices**
Ablation studies were conducted on a 1.7B-parameter model using the RULER benchmark to provide insights into specific design choices.
*   **Impact of State Size**: Increasing the fast weights size (by varying the number of TTT-enabled layers) consistently improved In-Place TTT performance. This suggests that larger fast weights facilitate more effective adaptation to contextual information, supporting the repurposing of MLP states.
*   **Impact of Chunk Size**: Optimal performance was observed with intermediate chunk sizes (e.g., 512 and 1024), indicating a trade-off between granularity of updates and parallelism efficiency. A chunk size of 1024 provided a balance of performance and efficiency.
*   **Impact of LM-Aligned Objective**: The LM-Aligned objective, defined by `V̂ = Conv1D(X₀)W_target`, was ablated. Both the `Conv1D` operator (for future token information) and the `W_target` projection matrix were found to be necessary for performance. `Conv1D` showed a critical role for long contexts, while `W_target` was crucial for short contexts, aligning with the theoretical analysis.
*   **Efficiency Impact**: In-Place TTT introduced negligible overhead in terms of prefill throughput and peak memory consumption when applied to 4B models with both SWA and Full Attention across various context lengths (8k to 128k).

Collectively, these findings suggest that In-Place TTT is an effective and scalable framework that enhances LLM capabilities for long-context processing and dynamic adaptation, both as a drop-in module for pre-trained models and when integrated from scratch.

---

### 6. Significance and Potential Impact

The In-Place Test-Time Training (In-Place TTT) framework presents a notable advancement in addressing the static nature of Large Language Models (LLMs) post-deployment. Its significance lies in providing a practical and effective method for enabling dynamic adaptation, which has previously been a considerable challenge for these large-scale models.

The potential impacts of this work include:

*   **Enabling Continual Learning in LLMs**: By allowing LLMs to update a subset of their parameters (fast weights) at inference time, In-Place TTT provides a mechanism for continuous learning from new data streams. This moves LLMs closer to a paradigm where they can dynamically internalize and utilize evolving contextual information, potentially enhancing their ability to handle long-horizon tasks and adapt to changing real-world scenarios more effectively.
*   **Practical Drop-in Enhancement**: The "in-place" design, which repurposes existing MLP blocks, represents a crucial contribution. It eliminates the need for costly and time-consuming pretraining from scratch, which has been a major barrier for integrating Test-Time Training (TTT) into established, billion-parameter LLMs. This architectural compatibility facilitates the widespread adoption of dynamic adaptation techniques by allowing for lightweight, on-the-fly enhancements to existing pre-trained models.
*   **Improved Efficiency and Scalability**: The development of a chunk-wise update mechanism that operates complementarily to the attention mechanism, permitting larger chunk sizes, addresses critical computational inefficiencies. Coupled with its Context Parallelism compatibility, In-Place TTT offers a scalable solution for high-throughput language modeling on modern hardware, making dynamic adaptation practical for real-world deployment.
*   **Tailored Language Model Adaptation**: The introduction of a theoretically-grounded, Next-Token Prediction (NTP)-aligned objective is significant. It moves beyond generic reconstruction tasks, focusing the fast weights on learning predictively useful information for autoregressive language modeling. This objective alignment is anticipated to lead to more effective contextual understanding and generation capabilities.
*   **Enhanced Long-Context Reasoning**: The experimental results, particularly the consistent performance gains on long-context benchmarks like RULER and improved perplexity for extended contexts, suggest that In-Place TTT can significantly bolster an LLM's capacity to process and reason over very long sequences of information. This could be particularly impactful for applications requiring deep contextual understanding, such as document summarization, complex code analysis, or scientific text processing.
*   **Foundation for Future Research**: The framework establishes a foundation for further exploration in dynamic LLM adaptation. The orthogonality of its core principles to specific loss functions, optimizers, and even efficient backbone architectures (like State-Space Models) suggests avenues for future work to combine In-Place TTT with other advancements, potentially leading to even more powerful and adaptive LLMs.

In summary, In-Place TTT represents a promising step towards resolving key limitations in current LLM architectures, offering a practical, efficient, and aligned approach to integrating dynamic, continual adaptation capabilities.
overview:
## Introduction to Dynamic Adaptation in LLMs

Large Language Models (LLMs) currently operate under a fixed paradigm: they are trained on massive datasets and then deployed as static entities. Once a model is deployed, its internal knowledge (stored in its "weights") remains unchanged. While this works well for many tasks, it creates a significant bottleneck when models encounter new information, extremely long documents, or evolving contexts.

In-Place Test-Time Training (In-Place TTT) introduces a framework designed to break this static limitation. By enabling a model to update its own parameters on the fly during inference—specifically for each new input it processes—In-Place TTT allows LLMs to adapt dynamically. Unlike previous attempts at test-time training that required entirely new model architectures or expensive retraining from scratch, this approach repurposes existing components within standard Transformer architectures.

![Figure 1: Overview of the In-Place TTT framework within a standard Transformer block.](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.06169v1/x1.png)
*Caption: The In-Place TTT framework repurposes the MLP block's final projection as "fast weights," updating them through a language-modeling-aligned objective during inference.*

The core principle involves designating a subset of the model's weights as "fast weights" which evolve as the model reads a sequence, while the remaining "slow weights" stay fixed. This transition from a purely static model to one that continuously learns during its own execution represents a shift toward more adaptive and context-aware artificial intelligence.

## The Static Nature of Modern Language Models

Most modern LLMs rely on the Attention mechanism to handle context. As a model processes a sequence of words (tokens), it stores information about past tokens in a memory structure called the Key-Value (KV) cache. While effective, this approach has two primary drawbacks:

1.  **Fixed Knowledge**: The model's fundamental understanding of how to process information is baked into weights that never change after the training phase. It cannot "learn" a new pattern or internalize a massive technical manual during a single conversation without filling up its context window.
2.  **Memory Bottleneck**: The KV cache grows linearly with the length of the input. For very long sequences (hundreds of thousands of tokens), the memory required to store the KV cache becomes prohibitive, and the computational cost of attending to every previous token increases quadratically or requires complex approximations.

Traditional Test-Time Training (TTT) was proposed as an alternative: instead of just storing tokens in a cache, the model could compress that information into a small set of auxiliary weights. However, early TTT methods often introduced specialized layers that required building and training models from the ground up, making them incompatible with established, high-performance models like Llama or Qwen.

## In-Place TTT: Repurposing the MLP Block

The fundamental contribution of the In-Place TTT framework is the concept of "in-place" adaptation. Instead of adding new layers, the researchers identified the Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) block—a standard component in every Transformer layer—as the perfect candidate for test-time updates.

In a standard Transformer, the MLP block typically follows a gated structure:
$$O = \phi(HW_{gate}^\top) \odot (HW_{up}^\top) W_{down}^\top$$
In this equation, $H$ represents the input activations, $\phi(\cdot)$ is an activation function (like SiLU), and $W_{gate}$, $W_{up}$, and $W_{down}$ are weight matrices.

In-Place TTT designates the $W_{down}$ matrix as the "fast weights." During inference, as the model processes a document, it performs a gradient descent step to update $W_{down}$ based on the specific information it is currently reading. Because $W_{down}$ is already a part of the pre-trained model, this enhancement can be applied as a "drop-in" modification to existing models. The pre-trained values of $W_{down}$ serve as a strong initialization, and the model merely refines them to better suit the immediate context.

## Scaling Inference with Chunk-Wise Updates

A major hurdle for any online learning method is computational efficiency. Standard training involves sequential updates: process a token, calculate the error, and update the weights. Doing this token-by-token during inference would be incredibly slow on modern GPUs, which are designed for massive parallel processing.

To solve this, In-Place TTT utilizes a **chunk-wise update strategy**. Rather than updating weights after every single token, the model processes tokens in groups (chunks), such as 512 or 1024 tokens at a time. The process works in two parallel steps:

1.  **Apply Operation**: The model uses the current version of the fast weights $W_{down}^{(i)}$ to calculate the output for the current chunk $i$.
2.  **Update Operation**: Simultaneously, the model calculates how the weights should change based on the information in the current chunk to prepare the weights $W_{down}^{(i+1)}$ for the *next* chunk.

This approach allows the model to maintain the high throughput required for real-world applications. By using a single step of gradient descent per chunk, the update rule becomes:
$$W_{down}^{(i+1)} = W_{down}^{(i)} - \eta \nabla_W L(Z[i](W_{down}^{(i)})^\top, V[i])$$
where $\eta$ is the learning rate, $Z[i]$ are the intermediate activations (acting as "keys"), and $V[i]$ are the targets (acting as "values").

## Language-Modeling-Aligned Learning Objectives

Most previous TTT research used a simple "reconstruction" task: the weights were updated so that they could recreate the current input token from its own representation. While this helps the model "remember" the context, it isn't specifically tailored to what language models actually do: predict the next token.

In-Place TTT introduces a **Next-Token Prediction (NTP) Aligned Objective**. Instead of forcing the fast weights to memorize the current state $X_t$, the framework encourages them to store information that helps predict the future state $X_{t+1}$. 

The target $V$ for the update is defined using a small, 1D convolutional layer and a projection:
$$\hat{V} = \text{Conv1D}(X_0)W_{target}$$
The $\text{Conv1D}(\cdot)$ operator allows the model to look slightly ahead or aggregate local context, ensuring that the updates to the fast weights are predictively useful. When this is combined with a linear loss function (the negative Frobenius inner product), the update rule simplifies into a highly efficient form:
$$W_{down}^{(i)} = W_{down}^{(i-1)} + \eta \hat{V}[i]^\top Z[i]$$
This mathematical form is associative, meaning it can be implemented using "parallel prefix sums," a technique that allows the model to calculate updates for many chunks across multiple GPUs simultaneously (Context Parallelism).

## Theoretical Foundation: Why Predictive Alignment Matters

The researchers provided a theoretical analysis to explain why predicting the next token is superior to simple reconstruction during test-time training. They focused on a classic benchmark for in-context learning: the "Induction Head." 

In an induction task, a model sees a pattern like `[A] [B] ... [A]` and must predict that `[B]` follows the second `[A]`. The theoretical analysis shows that with an NTP-aligned objective, the expected change in the model's confidence (logits) for the correct next token $v^*$ is guaranteed to increase:
$$E[\Delta\ell_n[v^*]] \geq \lambda_{lr} \cdot c_{norm}^2 \cdot c_{align}$$
where $\lambda_{lr}$ is related to the learning rate and $c_{align}$ measures how well the model's internal activations correlate. Conversely, they proved that a standard reconstruction objective provides negligible improvement for this predictive task, as it focuses only on the identity of the current token rather than the relationship between consecutive tokens.

## Experimental Success: Drop-in Improvements and Scratch Training

The researchers tested In-Place TTT in two scenarios: as a "drop-in" extension for existing models (like Qwen3-4B and Llama-3.1-8B) and as a core component of models trained from scratch.

### Drop-in Enhancement
When applied to Qwen3-4B, the model was continually trained on a small amount of data to learn how to use the TTT mechanism. On the **RULER benchmark**, which tests a model's ability to retrieve information from contexts up to 256,000 tokens long, In-Place TTT consistently outperformed the base model. At a context length of 128k, the TTT-enhanced model achieved 77.0% accuracy compared to the baseline's 74.8%. More importantly, the gap widened as the sequences became longer, proving that the fast weights effectively compressed and managed long-range information.

### Pre-training from Scratch
In models trained from scratch at 500M and 1.5B parameter scales, In-Place TTT was compared against other efficient architectures like Gated Linear Attention (GLA) and State-Space Models (SSMs). 

![Figure 2: Validation perplexity for 500M models.](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.06169v1/x2.png)
![Figure 3: Validation perplexity for 1.5B models.](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.06169v1/x3.png)
*Caption: In-Place TTT achieves lower (better) perplexity across various context lengths compared to other efficient attention and TTT methods.*

As seen in Figures 2 and 3, In-Place TTT achieved the lowest perplexity (a measure of prediction error) across context lengths ranging from 2k to 32k tokens. This indicates that the framework is not just a patch for existing models but a competitive architecture for long-context language modeling in its own right.

## Efficiency and Design Choices

A key concern with adding training steps to the inference process is the "prefill" speed—how fast the model can process an initial prompt. The researchers demonstrated that because of the chunk-wise parallel implementation, the overhead is negligible. For a 4B model processing 128k tokens, the memory usage and tokens-per-second remained nearly identical to the standard Transformer baseline.

![Figure 4: Ablation studies on state size, chunk size, and the learning objective.](https://paper-assets.alphaxiv.org/figures-normalized/figures/2604.06169v1/x4.png)
*Caption: Larger state sizes (more TTT layers) and the inclusion of the LM-Aligned objective (Conv and Proj) are critical for achieving high performance on long-context tasks.*

Ablation studies (Figure 4) highlighted several critical findings:
*   **State Size**: The more layers enabled with TTT, the better the performance. This suggests that the model benefits from having more "fast weight" capacity to store contextual information.
*   **Chunk Size**: A chunk size of 1024 tokens provided the best balance between update granularity and hardware efficiency.
*   **Objective**: Removing the 1D Convolution (which provides future token info) or the projection matrix significantly degraded performance, especially at longer context lengths.

## Conclusion and Significance

In-Place Test-Time Training represents a practical path toward adaptive Large Language Models. By repurposing the existing MLP blocks and introducing a predictive learning objective, it enables models to "think" and "learn" as they read, without requiring the massive memory overhead of an infinite KV cache or the prohibitive cost of building new architectures from scratch.

This work bridges the gap between static, pre-trained models and the need for dynamic, long-horizon reasoning. As AI applications move toward processing entire codebases, legal libraries, and scientific corpora, the ability for a model to adapt its own weights in real-time will likely become an essential feature of the next generation of intelligent systems.

keyInsights: Existing MLP blocks in Transformer architectures can be effectively repurposed as 'fast weights' for dynamic adaptation, avoiding architectural modifications and costly pretraining from scratch.
Decoupling Test-Time Training updates from the attention mechanism allows for significantly more efficient chunk-wise processing, leveraging hardware parallelism while maintaining performance.

An objective specifically aligned with Next-Token Prediction significantly enhances the effectiveness of fast weights for autoregressive language models, as theoretically shown to increase logits for correct future tokens.
originalProblem: Large Language Models operate under a static 'train then deploy' paradigm, preventing dynamic adaptation to new information or evolving contexts during inference.
Existing Test-Time Training (TTT) methods for dynamic adaptation often introduce specialized layers incompatible with standard LLM architectures, requiring prohibitive pretraining.
Canonical TTT update rules are computationally inefficient due to sequential per-token updates and misaligned generic self-supervised objectives for autoregressive language modeling.
results: In-Place TTT consistently improved RULER benchmark scores for pre-trained LLMs (Qwen3-4B/14B, LLaMA-3.1-8B), showing up to +2.7% gain at 64k context lengths and maintaining advantage during extrapolation to 256k context.
Models pre-trained from scratch with In-Place TTT achieved lower validation perplexity than all competitive baselines across varying context lengths (2k to 32k) on Pile and Proof-Pile-2 datasets.
The framework introduced negligible overhead in prefill throughput and peak memory consumption, demonstrating its practicality and scalability for integration into LLMs.
solution: In-Place TTT repurposes existing Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) blocks within Transformer architectures, specifically designating the final projection matrix (W_down) as adaptable 'fast weights' for in-place updates, enabling a 'drop-in' enhancement.
An efficient chunk-wise update strategy is implemented, decoupling TTT from attention to allow for larger chunk sizes and leverage modern parallel hardware without compromising performance.

A novel, theoretically-grounded learning objective is introduced, explicitly tailored for the Next-Token Prediction (NTP) task of language modeling, encouraging fast weights to store predictively useful information.
summary.summary: In-Place Test-Time Training (In-Place TTT) enables dynamic adaptation in large language models by repurposing existing MLP blocks for efficient, chunk-wise updates with a next-token prediction aligned objective. This framework demonstrates consistent performance improvements on long-context benchmarks, such as RULER, and achieves lower perplexity compared to baselines, while introducing negligible computational overhead.

title: In-Place Test-Time Training
